Text-to-Speech

DEPARTMENT 205 LAW AND MOTION RULINGS


Case Number: 22SMCV01940    Hearing Date: March 3, 2026    Dept: 205

Superior Court of California 

County of Los Angeles – West District 

Beverly Hills Courthouse / Department 205 

 

 

GAVIN POLONE AS TRUSTEE OF THE GAVIN POLONE TRUST,  

  

Plaintiff, 

v. 

 

SHWMUT WOODWORKING & SUPPLY, INC., et al.,  

 

Defendants. 

 

  Case No.: 22SMCV01940 

  

  Hearing Date: March 3, 2026 

  

  [TENTATIVE] ORDER RE: 

  CROSS-COMPLAINANT AND CROSS- 

  DEFENDANT JRC GROUP, INC.’S  

  MOTION FOR GOOD FAITH  

  SETTLEMENT 

 

 

BACKGROUND 

 

This is a construction defect case.  Plaintiff Gavin Polone as Trustee of the Gavin Polone Trust sued its general contractor Defendant Shawmut. (“Shawmut”) for work done on two properties, 2401 Briar Crest Rd., Beverly Hills, CA 90210 and 1975 Carla Ridge Drive, Beverly Hills, CA 90210 (the Property).  Plaintiff brought claims for fraud, negligent misrepresentation, breach of contract, negligence, common count and accounting.     

Shawmut then filed a cross-complaint against JRC Group, Inc., a company Plaintiff hired to perform repairs.  Shawmut alleged that JRC demolished and replaced a significant portion of Shawmut’s work and that it did so negligently resulting in significant damage to areas previously constructed by Shawmut.  Shawmut alleged a single claim for equitable indemnity against JRC.  It should be noted that Plaintiff never sued JRC or alleged it was negligent in any way. 

JRC then cross-complained against PRS Inc. and Allied Roofing & Waterproofing Inc., alleging claims for indemnity, contribution, apportionment and declaratory relief.  JRC alleged that if there was any damage to the Property, it was caused by these cross-defendants.   

JRC has now settled with Plaintiff for $10,000.  The settlement was reached with the aid of a mediator, Jerry Kurland, Esq.   

This hearing is on JRC’s motion for determination of good faith settlement.  JRC argues that based on the fact that Plaintiff does not claim any liability by JRC, JRC’s settlement of $10,000 is unmistakably in good faith.  Shawmut opposes the motion, and Cross-Defendant Sound-Crete Construction Inc. joins in the opposition.   

LEGAL STANDARD 

Under Code of Civil Procedure § 877.6, “[a]ny party to an action in which it is alleged that two or more parties are joint tortfeasors or co-obligors on a contract debt shall be entitled to a hearing on the issue of the good faith of a settlement entered into by the plaintiff or other claimant and one or more alleged tortfeasors or co-obligors.”  (Code Civ. Proc.§ 877.6(a)(1).)  “The issue of the good faith of a settlement may be determined by the court on the basis of affidavits served with the notice of hearing, and any counter-affidavits filed in response, or the court may, in its discretion, receive other evidence at the hearing.” (Code Civ. Proc.§ 877.6(b).)   

Section 877.6 requires “that the courts review [settlement] agreements made under its aegis to insure that the settlements appropriately balance the . . . statute’s dual objectives” of (1) providing an “equitable sharing of costs among the parties at fault” and (2) encouraging parties to resolve their disputes by way of settlement.¿ (Tech-Bilt, 38 Cal.3d at 494.) 

“A determination by the court that the settlement was made in good faith shall bar any other joint tortfeasor or co-obligor from any further claims against the settling tortfeasor or co-obligor for equitable comparative contribution, or partial or comparative indemnity, based on comparative negligence or comparative fault.”  (Code Civ. Proc.§ 877.6(c).)  The party asserting the lack of good faith shall have the burden of proof on that issue.  (Code Civ. Proc.§ 877.6(d).)   

In Tech-Bilt, the California Supreme Court set forth the following factors for evaluating whether a proposed settlement was made in good faith: (1) a rough approximation of plaintiffs’ total recovery and the settler’s proportionate liability; (2) the amount paid in settlement; (3) the allocation of settlement proceeds among plaintiffs; (4) discount for settlement before trial; (5) the financial conditions and insurance policy limits of settling defendants; and (6) the existence of collusion, fraud, or tortious conduct aimed to injure the interests of non-settling defendants.  (38 Cal.3d at 499.)  The Court considers each of these factors below. 

DISCUSSION 

A motion for determination of good faith settlement must be based on admissible, competent evidence.  (Brehm Cmtys. v. Superior Court (2001) 88 Cal.App.4th 730, 736.)  Here, the only evidence submitted by JRC is the declaration of its counsel.  Counsel does not have foundation to attest to the nature of JRC’s work on the Property and therefore, its proportionate liability.  Accordingly, the Court denies the motion for determination of good faith settlement. 

CONCLUSION 

Based on the foregoing, the Court DENIES the motion for determination of a good faith settlement.   

IT IS SO ORDERED. 

 

DATED: March 3, 2026 ___________________________ 

Edward B. Moreton, Jr. 

Judge of the Superior Court 

Case Number: 25SMCV01132    Hearing Date: March 3, 2026    Dept: 205

HEARING DATE:  March 3, 2026 

JUDGE/DEPT:  Moreton/Beverly Hills, 205 

CASE NAME: Porsche Leasing Ltd. et al. v. Tsisana Mikia, et al. 

CASE NUMBER:  25SMCV01132 

 

COMP. FILED: March 6, 2025 

 

PROCEEDINGS: REQUEST FOR ENTRY OF DEFAULT JUDGMENT 

MOVING PARTY: Porsche Leasing Ltd. and Porsche Financial Services, Inc. 

RESPONDING PARTY: Tsisana Mikia 

BACKGROUND 

This action arises from the breach of a lease agreement for a new 2023 Porsche Panamera, Vehicle Identification Number WP0AJ2A72PL103860 (“Vehicle”). Defendant Tsisana Mikia defaulted on the agreement by failing to make the required payments.  

Plaintiff Porsche Leasing Ltd. is the owner of the Vehicle, and Plaintiff Porsche Financial Services, Inc. is the servicer of the account.  They have brought this action to obtain a Judgement for Possession and monetary damages due to the breach.   

The Vehicle has since been sold in an auction to satisfy a lien by Black & White Towing.  Plaintiffs have not received any proceeds from the sale and, as a result of the auction, no longer have an ownership interest in the Vehicle.  

Plaintiff served Defendant by order of publication.  Defendant was obligated to respond but failed to do so.  Plaintiff successfully requested the entry of Defendant’s default, which was entered by the Clerk’s Office on November 20, 2025.  Plaintiff requested a default judgment on January 26, 2026.  Plaintiff served a copy of the request for default judgment on Defendant.     

 

RELIEF REQUESTED 

 

Default judgment against Defendant for a total of $119,980.67, which is comprised of: (1) $103,643.87, for damages, (2) $12,183.60 for interest, (3) $2,926.44 for attorneys’ fees, and (4) $1,226.75, for costs.  

 

ANALYSIS 

 

Code Civ. Proc. § 585 sets forth the two options for obtaining a default judgment. First, where the plaintiff’s complaint¿seeks compensatory damages only, in a sum certain which is readily ascertainable from the allegations of the complaint or statement of damages, the clerk may enter the default judgment for that amount. However, if the relief requested in the complaint is more complicated, consisting of either nonmonetary relief, or monetary relief in amounts which require either an accounting, additional evidence, or the exercise of judgment to ascertain, the plaintiff must request entry of judgment by the court. In such cases, the plaintiff must affirmatively establish his entitlement to the specific judgment requested.¿ (Kim v. Westmoore Partners, Inc. (2011) 201 Cal.App.4th 267, 287.)  Section 585 also allows for interest, costs and attorney fees, where otherwise allowed by law. (Code Civ. Proc., § 585(a).) 

 

Multiple specific documents are required, such as: (1) form CIV 100, (2) a brief summary of the case; (3) declarations or other admissible evidence in support of the judgment requested; (4) interest computations as necessary; (5) a memorandum of costs and disbursements; (6) a proposed form of judgment; (7) a dismissal of all parties against whom judgment is not sought or an application for separate judgment under Code Civ. Proc. § 579, supported by a showing of grounds for each judgment; (8) exhibits as necessary; and (9) a request for attorneys’ fees if allowed by statute or by the agreement of the parties.  (CRC Rule 3.1800.)  

Here, Plaintiffs have properly complied with all the substantive and procedural requirements for a default judgment. Substantively, Plaintiffs declare there have been damages in the amount of $103,643.87.  (Farmer Decl., paragraph 9.)  The evidence submitted (the lease agreement and payment history) is authenticated by declaration.  The costs Plaintiffs seek are itemized in Item 7 of the CIV 100 form and are also supported by declaration.  (Farmer Decl., paragraph 18.)  Plaintiffs’ request for attorneys’ fees is authorized by the parties’ agreement, and the amount of fees is supported by declaration.  (Farmer Decl., paragraphs 15-16.)  Plaintiffs’ request for interest is authorized by statute, and Plaintiffs have provided an accounting of the amount of interest they seek.  (Farmer Decl., paragraph 17.)  Procedurally, Plaintiffs properly served Defendant more than 30 days prior to requesting entry of default and default judgment, correctly completed JC Form CIV-100 in a manner that would not void or put at issue the entry of default, provided a declaration of non-military status, requested damages in amounts supported by the filings and not in excess of the amount stated in the Complaint (see Compl. paragraph 10), and filed a proposed judgment (JUD-100).  As default has already been entered and there has been no appearance by Defendant, default judgment is appropriate here.  

 

CONCLUSION AND ORDER  

 

For the foregoing reasons, Plaintiffs’ Request for Default Judgment is GRANTED.  Default judgment in the amount of $119,980.67 is entered against Defendant.
